questions.question:
Which one of the following is NOT performed during compilation?
A. Dynamic memory allocation
B. Type checking
C. Symbol table management
D. Inline expansion
questions.to_find: Which activity is NOT part of the compilation process?

understand.plain: A compiler translates source code to machine code and performs many tasks like type checking, managing symbols, and expanding inline functions. However, some tasks happen only when the program actually runs (at runtime), not during compilation. Dynamic memory allocation (e.g., malloc/new) is decided and executed at runtime when the program needs memory.

keywords.explain: Requesting memory from the heap at program execution time, not at compile time.
keywords.example: int *p = malloc(10 * sizeof(int)); — the OS actually allocates this block when the program runs.

keywords.explain: A compiler optimization where a function call is replaced by the actual function body, eliminating call overhead.
keywords.example: inline int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; } — the compiler inserts the body wherever add() is called.

keywords.explain: The compiler builds and maintains a table of all identifiers (variables, functions) with their types and scopes during compilation.
keywords.example: When int x is declared, the compiler records 'x -> int' in the symbol table.

understand.visual_alt: Compiler pipeline: Source -> Lexer -> Parser -> Semantic Analysis (type checking, symbol table) -> Optimizer (inline expansion) -> Code Gen -> Machine Code. Dynamic memory allocation happens only at runtime.

given.aim: Identify which of the four listed activities does NOT happen during the compilation phase.

terms.meaning: Verifying that operands in expressions have compatible types.
terms.example: Flagging int x = "hello"; as a type error.
terms.connects: Performed by the semantic analysis phase of the compiler.

terms.meaning: Creating, populating, and querying the table of all declared names.
terms.example: Recording that function foo returns int.
terms.connects: Performed throughout lexical, parsing, and semantic analysis phases.

terms.meaning: Replacing a function call site with the callee's body during compilation.
terms.example: Compiler replaces max(a,b) with (a>b?a:b) directly in the code.
terms.connects: Performed by the compiler's optimizer before code generation.

given.plan: Go through each option and determine whether it is a compile-time or runtime activity. Dynamic memory allocation (malloc/new) involves requesting memory from the OS heap while the program runs — the compiler cannot know how much memory to allocate at compile time in general, so this is a runtime activity.

steps.title: Evaluate compile-time activities
steps.apply: Type checking (B): done during semantic analysis — compile time. Symbol table management (C): done throughout compilation phases — compile time. Inline expansion (D): done by optimizer during compilation — compile time.
steps.note: All three of B, C, D are well-known compile-time activities.

steps.title: Evaluate dynamic memory allocation
steps.apply: Dynamic memory allocation (A): calls like malloc() or new are executed at runtime. The compiler generates the call instruction but the actual memory is allocated by the runtime system/OS when the instruction executes. Hence it is NOT performed during compilation.
steps.note: Do not confuse static memory layout (which the compiler does compute) with dynamic heap allocation (which happens at runtime).

solution.result: A. Dynamic memory allocation — it is a runtime activity, not a compile-time activity.

questions.formula_note: Compile-time vs. runtime activity classification: dynamic memory allocation (malloc/new) is a runtime operation; type checking, symbol table management, and inline expansion are all compile-time operations.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements are CORRECT?
1. Static allocation of all data areas by a compiler makes it impossible to implement recursion.
2. Automatic garbage collection is essential to implement recursion.
3. Dynamic allocation of activation records is essential to implement recursion.
4. Both heap and stack are essential to implement recursion.

A. 1 and 2 only
B. 2 and 3 only
C. 3 and 4 only
D. 1 and 3 only
questions.to_find: Which statements about memory allocation and recursion are correct?

understand.plain: Recursion requires each active call of a function to have its own private copy of local variables and return address. If a compiler allocates data areas statically (fixed at compile time), all calls share the same memory, so recursion breaks. What is needed is dynamic allocation of activation records (stack frames) so each recursive call gets its own frame. Garbage collection is unrelated to enabling recursion, and the heap is not required — the stack alone is sufficient.

keywords.explain: A block of memory pushed onto the call stack for each function invocation, holding local variables, return address, and parameters.
keywords.example: When fib(5) calls fib(4), a new activation record for fib(4) is pushed on top of fib(5)'s record.

keywords.explain: Memory for variables is assigned at a fixed address at compile time — only one copy exists for each variable.
keywords.example: FORTRAN 77 used static allocation; it cannot support recursion because there is only one copy of each local variable.

keywords.explain: Automatic reclamation of heap memory no longer referenced by any live variable.
keywords.example: Java's GC reclaims objects that are no longer reachable; it is not needed to support recursion.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="340" height="220" viewBox="0 0 340 220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="140" height="180" class="bg"/><text x="80" y="30" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Call Stack</text><rect x="20" y="40" width="120" height="40" class="hi"/><text x="80" y="55" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">fib(3)</text><text x="80" y="72" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">AR: n=3, ret</text><rect x="20" y="85" width="120" height="40" class="hi"/><text x="80" y="100" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">fib(2)</text><text x="80" y="117" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">AR: n=2, ret</text><rect x="20" y="130" width="120" height="40" class="hi"/><text x="80" y="145" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">fib(1)</text><text x="80" y="162" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">AR: n=1, ret</text><text x="200" y="60" class="sub ink">Each recursive call</text><text x="200" y="80" class="sub ink">gets its OWN</text><text x="200" y="100" class="sub ink">activation record.</text><text x="200" y="130" class="sub ink">Static alloc = only</text><text x="200" y="150" class="sub ink">1 record shared</text><text x="200" y="170" class="sub ink">=> recursion fails.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A call stack diagram showing three stacked activation records for fib(3), fib(2), fib(1). Each has its own n and return address, illustrating that dynamic allocation of separate records is required for recursion.

given.aim: Determine which statements about static allocation, garbage collection, dynamic allocation, and recursion are true.

terms.term: Statement 1: Static allocation impossible for recursion
terms.meaning: With static allocation, each function has one fixed memory area; simultaneous recursive calls overwrite each other's data.
terms.example: FORTRAN 77 compilers using static allocation cannot compile recursive programs.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — static allocation prevents multiple live instances of the same function.

terms.term: Statement 2: Garbage collection essential for recursion
terms.meaning: GC frees heap memory automatically, but recursion uses the stack, not the heap.
terms.example: C programs implement recursion using the call stack without any GC.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — GC is not needed for recursion.

terms.term: Statement 3: Dynamic allocation of activation records essential
terms.meaning: Each function call must dynamically push a new activation record (stack frame) so multiple invocations coexist.
terms.example: The hardware call stack in every modern CPU does exactly this.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — without dynamically allocated frames, recursion cannot work.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against the known theory of runtime environments. Statement 4 claims heap is essential; but recursion uses the stack, not the heap, so statement 4 is false. Only 1 and 3 are correct.

steps.title: Check Statement 1 and 3
steps.apply: Statement 1: Static allocation assigns a single fixed address to each local variable of a function. When a function calls itself recursively, all active calls would share the same storage, corrupting each other's data. Hence recursion is impossible under pure static allocation. TRUE. Statement 3: Dynamic allocation of activation records (pushing/popping stack frames) allows each invocation to have private storage. This is exactly what modern compilers do using the runtime stack. TRUE.
steps.note: The key insight: recursion requires multiple simultaneous live instances of the same function, each needing private local data.

steps.title: Check Statement 2 and 4
steps.apply: Statement 2: Automatic garbage collection manages heap objects. Recursion is implemented via the call stack, not the heap. Languages like C implement recursion without any GC. FALSE. Statement 4: Only the stack is essential for recursion; the heap is not required. Purely stack-based runtimes (e.g., C, assembly) support recursion without a heap. FALSE.
steps.note: Heap and GC are useful for dynamic data structures but are orthogonal to the fundamental requirement for recursion.

solution.result: D. 1 and 3 only — static allocation prevents recursion (stmt 1 true) and dynamic activation records are essential (stmt 3 true).

questions.formula_note: Runtime environment theory: recursion requires dynamically allocated activation records (stack frames) so each call instance has private storage; static allocation (one fixed copy) makes recursion impossible; garbage collection and heap are not required for recursion.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements.
S_1: The sequence of procedure calls corresponds to a preorder traversal of the activation tree.
S_2: The sequence of procedure returns corresponds to a postorder traversal of the activation tree.

Which one of the following options is correct?
A. S_1 is true and S_2 is false
B. S_1 is false and S_2 is true
C. S_1 is true and S_2 is true
D. S_1 is false and S_2 is false
questions.answer: C. S_1 is true and S_2 is true
questions.to_find: Whether procedure calls correspond to preorder and procedure returns correspond to postorder traversal of the activation tree.

understand.plain: The activation tree is a tree where each node represents one function call, and children represent calls made from that function. When a program runs, it calls functions in a top-down (root-to-leaf) order — which is exactly preorder traversal. Returns happen bottom-up (leaf-to-root) — which matches postorder traversal. Both statements are therefore correct.

keywords.explain: A tree representing all procedure activations during a program run; the root is main(), and each node's children are the procedures called by that node in order.
keywords.example: If main calls f which calls g, the tree is main -> f -> g.

keywords.explain: Visit the node before visiting its children: root, then left subtree, then right subtree.
keywords.example: For tree A -> (B, C): preorder visits A, B, C — matching the order in which calls are made.

keywords.explain: Visit all children before the parent: left subtree, right subtree, then root.
keywords.example: For tree A -> (B, C): postorder visits B, C, A — matching the order in which returns happen (innermost first).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="380" height="240" viewBox="0 0 380 240"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx="190" cy="35" rx="40" ry="18" class="hi"/><text x="190" y="41" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">main</text><line x1="150" y1="53" x2="90" y2="100" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="230" y1="53" x2="290" y2="100" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><ellipse cx="90" cy="115" rx="35" ry="18" class="bg"/><text x="90" y="121" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">f()</text><ellipse cx="290" cy="115" rx="35" ry="18" class="bg"/><text x="290" y="121" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">g()</text><line x1="70" y1="133" x2="50" y2="175" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="110" y1="133" x2="130" y2="175" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><ellipse cx="50" cy="188" rx="30" ry="16" class="bg"/><text x="50" y="194" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">f1()</text><ellipse cx="130" cy="188" rx="30" ry="16" class="bg"/><text x="130" y="194" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">f2()</text><text x="190" y="160" class="sub ok" text-anchor="middle">Calls: main,f,f1,f2,g</text><text x="190" y="180" class="sub ok" text-anchor="middle">(preorder)</text><text x="190" y="205" class="sub no" text-anchor="middle">Returns: f1,f2,f,g,main</text><text x="190" y="225" class="sub no" text-anchor="middle">(postorder)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Activation tree with main at root, calling f() and g() as children. f() calls f1() and f2(). Call sequence (preorder): main, f, f1, f2, g. Return sequence (postorder): f1, f2, f, g, main.

given.aim: Verify whether procedure call order = preorder traversal and return order = postorder traversal of the activation tree.

terms.meaning: A procedure is 'visited' (entered) before any of its callees, matching preorder (process node before children).
terms.example: main is called before f, and f is called before f1 — this is root-before-children order.
terms.connects: Preorder visits root then left then right; procedure calls follow the same order since a function runs top-down.

terms.meaning: A procedure returns after all procedures it called have returned, matching postorder (process node after all children).
terms.example: f1 and f2 return before f returns — children complete before parent.
terms.connects: Postorder visits all children before the parent; returns follow the same nested structure of the call stack.

terms.meaning: The tree captures nesting: a child node's lifetime is strictly contained within its parent's lifetime.
terms.example: If f calls g, g's activation record is pushed after f's and popped before f's.
terms.connects: This nesting property is exactly what makes calls = preorder and returns = postorder.

given.plan: Reason from the definition of the activation tree and the two traversal orders. Since each procedure is called before any of its callees and returns after all callees return, S_1 and S_2 are both true.

steps.title: Verify S_1: calls correspond to preorder
steps.apply: In program execution, when a procedure P is entered (called), we 'visit' node P in the activation tree. Only after P starts running does it make calls to its children (callees). So the sequence of calls is: P is entered, then P's callees are entered in order. This matches preorder traversal exactly (process the node, then recurse on children). S_1 is TRUE.
steps.note: Preorder = root first, then children left-to-right — this maps directly to the order procedures begin executing.

steps.title: Verify S_2: returns correspond to postorder
steps.apply: A procedure P can return only after all procedures it called have returned (because the call stack requires inner frames to be popped first). This means all children of P in the activation tree complete before P completes. This is exactly postorder traversal (process all children before the parent). S_2 is TRUE.
steps.note: Postorder = children first, then parent — this is enforced by the LIFO (last-in, first-out) nature of the call stack.

solution.result: C. S_1 is true and S_2 is true — both traversal correspondences hold for any program's activation tree.

questions.formula_note: Activation tree traversal property: the sequence of procedure calls is a preorder traversal of the activation tree, and the sequence of procedure returns is a postorder traversal. This follows directly from the LIFO (stack-based) nature of procedure call/return.
